description: Architecture: introduce Redis as a cache-aside layer with TTL=5min; the TTL is part of the architecture, not a separate convention

conversation.title: Adding a cache layer in front of the user-profile service

turns.text: user-profile-service is the read-hot tier — every page load fans out to it. Postgres is fine but we're paying for read-replicas mostly to soak that traffic. I want to put a cache in front. What pattern, what TTL?

Cache-aside is the right pattern here: the application checks Redis first, falls back to Postgres on miss, populates Redis on the way back. Avoid read-through / write-through proxies — they add a failure mode where the cache becomes a SPOF. On TTL: profile data changes rarely but consistency-sensitively (a profile-name change should be visible within minutes, not hours). 5 minutes is the sweet spot — short enough that staleness is bounded by lunch-break, long enough that ~99% of reads hit the cache during steady state.

Architecture decision: front user-profile-service with Redis as a cache-aside layer. TTL = 5 minutes. Cache key is `user-profile:v1:{user_id}`. On profile mutation the writer issues an explicit `DEL` so freshness is sub-second on the write path; the 5-minute TTL is just the upper bound for stale reads from missed invalidations.

Good. Two patterns to layer on once it's running: (1) jittered TTL (5 minutes ± 30 seconds) so the cache doesn't stampede when many keys expire simultaneously after a deploy; (2) a `cache-version` prefix in the key (`user-profile:v1:`) so a schema change is a single `redis-cli FLUSHALL` of v1 keys without breaking writers — looks like you've already done that one.

expected.note: ONE architecture memory. The TTL=5min and the explicit-DEL-on-write are *part of the architecture's structure*, NOT separate conventions or decisions. Trap: extractor splits into 'use Redis cache' + 'TTL=5min' as two memories.
